common_name: American Skunk Cabbage / Yellow Skunk Cabbage

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring directly into boggy soil or shallow water margins; keep roots continuously moist and never allow to dry out during establishment.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring into saturated boggy soil or at the shallow margins of a pond, keeping the crown at or just below the waterline. Choose a site with dappled light or morning sun and afternoon shade, as the massive leaves can scorch in intense direct sun. Bright yellow spathes emerge from late winter onward before the foliage, providing important early-season color; the mild skunk odor fades quickly in open air. This slow-maturing native is a long-term landscape investment — allow several years for plants to settle and produce their full, architectural leaf canopy.

common_name: Asian Skunk Cabbage / White Skunk Cabbage

planting.note: Set rhizome divisions into boggy, humus-rich soil at pond or stream margins in early spring; maintain constant moisture and protect from desiccation throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant divisions into perpetually moist, organically rich soil at the edges of a shaded pond, stream, or constructed bog garden, setting the crown level with the soil surface. Pure white spathes appear in early spring before the large, glossy leaves emerge, making this a refined and elegant alternative to its American relative. This species is somewhat smaller and more compact, making it well suited to mid-sized water garden settings where Lysichiton americanus would overwhelm the space. Patience is essential — established clumps improve year over year, eventually forming impressive foliage masses that persist through summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring at the shallow margins of a wildlife pond or bog, setting the rhizome horizontally just below the water surface or in permanently saturated soil.

directions: Position rhizome divisions horizontally at the boggy margins of a pond or slow-moving stream, with the rhizome just at or barely below the waterline and roots anchored in mud. Small white spathes reminiscent of miniature calla lilies appear in late spring and early summer, followed by tightly clustered bright red berries that are highly attractive to wildlife. This circumpolar native spreads gradually by rhizome, slowly colonizing suitable wet margins without becoming invasive in cold-climate gardens. It thrives in the coldest zones where many aquatic ornamentals cannot survive, making it invaluable for northern and mountain water gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions into shallow water or saturated marsh soil after soil temperatures reach 65°F; place in full sun for maximum growth and flowering.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun into the shallow margins of a pond, lake, or constructed wetland, setting the crown just at or below the waterline in 2–6 inches of water or in constantly saturated soil. The tall, arching, banana-like leaves create dramatic vertical structure, and long drooping flower stalks bearing small violet blooms attract hummingbirds and native bees throughout the warm season. In zones 8–9 plants may die back to the roots after frost but reliably resprout from the rhizome as temperatures warm; in zones 10–11 they remain evergreen. This southeastern US and tropical native is an excellent large-scale accent for rain gardens, retention ponds, and naturalistic water features.

planting.note: Install plugs or sod in spring once soil has warmed to at least 60°F; space plugs 12–18 inches apart and water consistently through the first season to establish deep roots.

directions: Install plugs or sod sections in spring into well-drained, full-sun soil, spacing plugs 12–18 inches apart; plugs will spread via stolons to fill gaps within one to two growing seasons. Once established, this fine-bladed native grass of the Great Plains requires minimal irrigation, surviving and thriving on natural rainfall across most of its range, though an occasional deep watering during extended drought maintains better color and density. Mow once or twice per season to 3–4 inches if a tidy appearance is desired, or leave unmowed for a naturalistic, soft-textured meadow effect; the blue-green foliage turns a warm straw color during winter dormancy and greens up reliably each spring. Avoid heavy foot traffic during dormancy and do not over-fertilize, as excess nitrogen encourages weeds and diminishes the natural competitive advantage of this adapted native.
